scope: Stage 1 methodology for generalized volumetric defect detection using a CAD STL reference and a TIFF-stack scan. The recommendations are dataset-agnostic; no project dataset files were accessed.

methodology_design_constraints.processing_strategy: Stream TIFF slices or process bounded chunks; use NumPy/SciPy-style array operations, connected components, distance transforms, and graph tables instead of per-voxel Python loops.
memory_policy: Read scan metadata and representative slices before allocating full-volume arrays.
Prefer compact boolean/unsigned masks and chunk-wise intermediate results.
Persist only required intermediates: material mask, void mask, skeleton/graph records, and defect table.
Record voxel spacing and preserve physical coordinates throughout registration and measurement.

modality_roles..stl: Nominal geometry, expected feature topology, reference coordinate frame, and feature-level correspondence.
modality_roles..tif_stack: Measured grayscale attenuation volume from which material, discontinuity, and void evidence are derived.

methods.name: Calibrated industrial or micro-XCT acquisition
methods.purpose: Acquire a quantitative 3D attenuation volume that preserves thin struts, nodes, pores, and gaps.
core_steps: Select magnification, voltage/current, filtration, projections, and exposure from attenuation and smallest feature.
Apply documented detector, beam-hardening, and scatter corrections; reconstruct with recorded settings.
Record voxel pitch, field of view, orientation, and artefact-based image-quality checks.
Estimate practical probability of detection before interpreting small defects.
strengths: Non-destructive internal and external inspection
Supports metrology and defect inspection
Defines the coordinate system for all downstream measurements
limitations: Blur, partial volume, motion, and regularization can hide thin features.
Beam hardening, scatter, rings, and roughness bias segmentation.
A scan adequate for pores may be inadequate for strut metrology.
methods.compute_cost: Medium reconstruction cost; storage and I/O can dominate.
methods.visualization_needed: Yes: slices, attenuation histogram, artefact views, and volume rendering.

sources.title: Standard Guide for Computed Tomography
sources.method_support: CT principles, performance parameters, and interpretation.

sources.title: X-ray Microcomputed Tomography in Additive Manufacturing: A Review of the Current Technology and Applications
sources.method_support: Micro-CT acquisition and AM inspection applications.

sources.title: Validation of x-Ray Computed Tomography Detection Limits for Stochastic Flaws in Additively Manufactured Ti-6Al-4V
sources.method_support: Detection-limit and validation framing for AM flaws.

methods.name: Conservative preprocessing and material/void segmentation
methods.purpose: Separate material from air and, when justified, internal pores from material without erasing real gaps.
core_steps: Crop to the specimen while retaining its boundary.
Apply documented denoising and ring/beam-hardening correction conservatively.
Use global or locally adaptive thresholding calibrated on representative regions.
Remove only confirmed noise; morphology must not delete thin struts or seal true breaks.
For difficult pores, use supervised 2D/3D models only with held-out specimens and uncertainty reporting.
strengths: Thresholding is transparent and fast.
3D context reduces slice inconsistency.
ML can handle low contrast and appearance variation.
limitations: Partial-volume voxels make geometry threshold-dependent.
Roughness and density gradients mimic thin/thick regions.
ML needs representative labels and may fail under scanner/material shift.
methods.compute_cost: Low-medium for thresholding; high for 3D model training/inference.
methods.visualization_needed: Yes: mask overlays, threshold sweeps, and 3D material/void renderings.

sources.title: Porosity segmentation in X-ray computed tomography scans of metal additively manufactured specimens with machine learning
sources.method_support: ML segmentation of AM porosity and specialist-agreement validation.

sources.title: Automatic Volumetric Segmentation of Additive Manufacturing Defects with 3D U-Net
sources.method_support: 3D volumetric defect segmentation for small, low-contrast defects.

sources.title: New Test Method for Additive Manufacturing of Metals -- Non-destructive testing and evaluation -- Porosity Measurement with X-ray CT
sources.method_support: Reference-artefact calibration and traceable XCT porosity measurement.

methods.name: Topology-preserving skeletonization and graph extraction
methods.purpose: Convert the segmented lattice into nodes and edges for connectivity and strut-level analysis.
required_inputs: optional CAD topology or expected nodes
core_steps: Use a 3D medial-axis or topology-preserving thinning method with explicit connectivity conventions.
Prune only short low-persistence branches attributable to noise.
Detect branch points/endpoints and collapse node neighborhoods into graph nodes.
Trace paths between nodes as strut edges.
Compare component count, node degree, edge presence, and path continuity to expected topology; retain the mask as geometry authority.
strengths: Makes connectivity and missing/broken members explicit.
Efficiently summarizes repeated unit cells.
Supports orientation, deformation, and node-degree metrics.
limitations: Sensitive to segmentation noise, anisotropy, roughness, and node geometry.
Naive thinning creates spurious branches.
Threshold bridges can make a broken strut appear connected.
methods.compute_cost: Medium-high CPU/memory for large masks; graph construction is low.
methods.visualization_needed: Yes: skeleton overlaid on slices/volume and a status-colored graph.

sources.title: Building Skeleton Models via 3-D Medial Surface Axis Thinning Algorithms
sources.method_support: 3D thinning with local connectivity and Euler-characteristic preservation.

sources.title: Geometric and Topological Analysis of Three-Dimensional Porous Media: Pore Space Partitioning Based on Morphological Skeletonization
sources.method_support: Connectivity-preserving skeletonization and node/link classification.

sources.title: Evaluation of rods deformation of metal lattice structure in additive manufacturing based on skeleton extraction technology
sources.method_support: CT reconstruction and skeleton extraction for lattice-rod deformation.

methods.name: Distance-transform strut thickness measurement
methods.purpose: Estimate local radius/diameter and detect thin, thick, or necked regions along each strut.
core_steps: Compute an anisotropic Euclidean distance transform from material to background in physical units.
Sample distances on the centerline; local diameter is approximately twice the centerline distance.
Exclude or separately model node blends and endpoints.
Flag sustained deviations over a minimum physical run length, not single voxels.
Repeat over perturbed thresholds or with surface measurements to estimate uncertainty.
strengths: Fast, interpretable, and useful on curved struts.
Avoids fitting a cylinder to every member.
Provides a local process-gradient signal.
limitations: Biased for non-circular, rough, tapered, or intersecting struts.
Node overlap needs separate treatment.
All errors in the mask and voxel spacing propagate to thickness.
methods.compute_cost: Low-medium; distance transform is linear in voxel count.
methods.visualization_needed: Yes: radius-colored centerlines and edge profiles.

sources.title: Skeletonization and distance transforms of 3D volumes using graphics hardware
sources.authors: van Dortmont, van de Wetering, Telea
sources.method_support: 3D distance transforms and skeleton computation.

sources.title: Medial axis example: skeletonize
sources.authors: scikit-image developers; based on Lee et al.
sources.method_support: Medial-axis distance as a local width estimate.

sources.title: Image-Based Geometrical Characterization of Nodes in Additively Manufactured Lattice Structures

methods.name: Rule-based missing, broken, thin, thick, and bent-strut classification
methods.purpose: Produce auditable per-strut defect records from graph, geometry, and registered-CAD evidence.
outputs: defect class, severity, confidence, and coordinates per edge/node
core_steps: Missing: expected CAD edge has no matching centerline/material support.
Broken: expected edge is split, has a material-support gap, or terminates away from a node.
Thin/thick: sustained robust diameter excursion crosses a calibrated band.
Bent: registered centerline deviates from nominal axis by distance, angle, or curvature tolerance.
Use confirmed, probable, and review-required confidence tiers because classes can overlap.
strengths: Transparent and auditable for an MVP.
Combines topology and metrology.
Produces actionable per-strut records.
limitations: Needs nominal topology and engineering tolerances.
Defect classes can overlap.
Thresholds depend on process and scan resolution.
methods.compute_cost: Low after graph and profiles exist.
methods.visualization_needed: Yes: class-colored overlays and linked slice review.

sources.title: LatticeAnalytics: Strut-Level Visualization and Inspection of Additively Manufactured Lattice Structures
sources.method_support: End-to-end XCT lattice inspection with strut-level visualization.

sources.title: Computational modelling of strut defects in SLM manufactured lattice structures
sources.method_support: μCT-derived strut defects and geometric imperfections.

sources.title: Analysis of defects in additively manufactured lattice structures
sources.method_support: XCT geometric deviations grouped by orientation and statistically modeled.

methods.name: Defect spatial clustering and edge-versus-center analysis
methods.purpose: Identify random, clustered, build-direction, or specimen-boundary-associated defect patterns.
core_steps: Assign findings to unit cell, height, orientation, node/edge zone, and boundary-distance bin.
Compare counts with exposure-adjusted expected counts because edge bins may contain different numbers of members.
Use nearest-neighbor distances, DBSCAN/hierarchical clustering for exploration, and permutation or Ripley-type tests for inference.
Report bootstrap or specimen-level uncertainty and distinguish detection opportunity from occurrence.
strengths: Reveals process gradients invisible in global counts.
Works with voxel objects or graph-level records.
Supports targeted process feedback.
limitations: Depends on scale, edge definition, and detection bias.
One specimen gives weak statistical power.
Null models must account for periodic/nonuniform lattice geometry.
methods.visualization_needed: Yes: 3D point clouds, boundary-distance maps, and axial/orientation histograms.

sources.title: LatticeAnalytics: Strut-Level Visualization and Inspection of Additively Manufactured Lattice Structures
sources.method_support: Spatial and strut-level visual inspection of XCT lattice defects.

sources.title: Analysis of defects in additively manufactured lattice structures
sources.method_support: Orientation-grouped statistical analysis of XCT deviations.

methods.name: CT-to-CAD and CT-to-graph registration
methods.purpose: Match as-built XCT geometry to nominal CAD so each observed member has an expected edge and measurable deviation.
core_steps: Establish coarse orientation with build direction, envelope, fiducials, or robust datums.
Perform robust rigid registration with outlier rejection; do not let defects drive the transform.
Use periodicity and graph topology to associate edges.
Validate using independent datums, residual distributions, sampling sensitivity, and registration uncertainty.
strengths: Enables direct missing-edge and bend detection.
Separates global pose from local deviation.
Supports comparison across specimens and modalities.
limitations: Periodic symmetry can make matches ambiguous.
Roughness and defects bias unconstrained ICP-like methods.
Registration error may be comparable to the defect magnitude.
methods.compute_cost: Medium-high for point-cloud registration; graph matching is lower.
methods.visualization_needed: Yes: before/after overlays, residual heat maps, and matched edge IDs.

sources.title: Data registration for multi-method qualification of additive manufactured components
sources.method_support: Datum-based and refined sampling registration for XCT/CMM/CAD lattice qualification.

sources.title: Dimensional verification of metal additively manufactured lattice structures by X-ray computed tomography
sources.method_support: Traceable XCT dimensional verification and lattice metrology challenges.

sources.title: Exploring Registration of Optical, CMM and XCT for Verification of Supplemental Surfaces to Define AM Lattices
sources.method_support: Registration and supplemental-surface concepts for AM lattice metrology.

methods.name: 3D visualization and validation reporting
methods.purpose: Make automated findings auditable and verify each flag against grayscale, mask, graph, and CAD evidence.
core_steps: Synchronize orthogonal grayscale, mask, skeleton, CAD, and defect overlays.
Render material, graph, defects, and boundary-distance maps in 3D.
For each reviewed finding, show local slices plus edge ID, coordinates, dimensions, threshold, and confidence.
Validate with expert overlap, reference artefacts, designed flaws, CMM/optical checks, or sectioning when feasible.
Track repeatability across thresholds, operators, resampling, and repeated scans.
strengths: Exposes silent failures in segmentation, graph extraction, and registration.
Provides engineering evidence rather than only summary numbers.
Supports reproducibility and audit.
limitations: Occlusion and surface noise can mislead visual interpretation.
Visual agreement does not establish metrological uncertainty or probability of detection.
Interactive reports add storage and implementation cost.
methods.compute_cost: Low-medium for static figures; medium-high for interactive volume rendering.

sources.title: LatticeAnalytics: Strut-Level Visualization and Inspection of Additively Manufactured Lattice Structures
sources.method_support: Strut-level visual analytics for XCT lattices.

sources.title: Standard Guide for Computed Tomography
sources.method_support: Industrial CT performance and interpretation terminology.

recommended_minimum_viable_pipeline.goal: Auditable first-pass inspection of one lattice specimen.
steps: Acquire calibrated XCT at a voxel pitch appropriate to the smallest strut/defect; retain metadata and detection-limit evidence.
Conservatively correct/crop and threshold a material mask; perform threshold sensitivity checks without filling suspected gaps.
Compute a topology-preserving 3D skeleton and graph; compare components, node degrees, endpoints, and expected topology.
Register the graph/surface to nominal CAD using robust datums and constrained rigid alignment.
For each expected strut, classify missing/broken status, calculate distance-transform diameter profiles, and flag sustained thin/thick deviations; measure centerline-to-CAD bend deviation.
Map findings to unit cell, orientation, build height, and boundary distance; report simple clustering and edge-versus-center rates.
Generate slice-linked 2D/3D evidence for every flag and record thresholds, tolerances, residuals, confidence, and uncertainty.
Manually review representative normal/flagged members and, where feasible, compare selected dimensions or designed flaws with an independent method.

open_technical_risks: Resolution and partial volume can make the smallest feature non-detectable; a negative result is not proof of absence.
Threshold, beam hardening, roughness, and node overlap can create or erase apparent connections.
Skeleton topology and distance-transform thickness are unstable near nodes, rough surfaces, and resolution-limited gaps.
Periodic CAD symmetry can make registration ambiguous; unconstrained alignment can absorb real global distortion.
Thin, broken, and missing classes can overlap and require engineering tolerances and minimum run lengths.
One specimen has weak power for clustering and edge-versus-center inference.
ML methods require representative labels and may not transfer across material, process, scanner, or reconstruction settings.
Visualization supports review but cannot replace calibrated metrology, repeat scans, independent measurements, or probability-of-detection studies.
